questions.prompt: Why return 410 Gone (not 404) for sunsetted endpoints?

choices.text: Semantics matter for communicating intent. 404 means 'not found — maybe it never existed, maybe a typo'; consumers see this and check their code for bugs ('did I get the URL wrong?'), waste time debugging. 410 Gone means 'this resource USED to exist, intentionally removed' — different signal. Combined with a structured error body pointing to migration docs (`{"code": "endpoint_removed", "doc_url": "..."}`), the consumer knows immediately: 'oh, the endpoint was sunsetted; I need to update.' Saves debugging time on their end; reduces support tickets on yours. HTTP semantics carry meaning beyond 'success or not'; using the right status code is part of the API contract. Mature APIs handle deprecation lifecycle with Deprecation headers during warning period, then 410 with migration docs after sunset. Cheap signal; meaningfully helpful.

questions.explanation: 410 = 'used to exist, intentionally removed.' 404 looks like a typo; 410 communicates planned removal.

questions.prompt: You want to sunset a deprecated endpoint in 30 days. Most customers haven't migrated. Right move?

choices.text: Extend the deprecation period. Sunsetting a heavily-used endpoint causes broad consumer breakage — angry customers, support flood, churn, possible contractual issues for enterprise contracts. Better: postpone the sunset date, identify top users still on the deprecated endpoint, reach out personally with migration help, add aggressive in-product warnings (dashboard banners, email reminders), maybe offer migration support hours. Once usage has dropped to near-zero among customers who care, sunset is safe. For very large APIs (Stripe, GitHub, AWS), deprecation periods stretch to multiple years for exactly this reason. The 'we announced; you didn't move; tough' stance burns goodwill catastrophically and rarely accomplishes the supposed goal (the engineering cost of maintaining the old endpoint is usually small compared to the customer-relationship cost of breaking them). Compromise: phase out by traffic — return 410 for some percent of requests escalating monthly; gives last-chance warnings; catches consumers who ignored notifications. Hard cutoffs with broad-usage endpoints are mostly career-limiting moves for the responsible PM/engineer.

questions.explanation: Extend deprecation; reach out to top users; phase out by traffic; sunset when usage is near zero.
